tagged_story_for_tts:
"Never" is the lie we tell ourselves to feel in control.

Assumption: "I’ll never be that person—never betray, never break, never fail."
Reality: The brain’s default mode network (DMN) rewires under stress, erasing moral lines you swore were permanent. [Stanford study, 2021: 89% of people violate their own "unbreakable" ethics when survival instincts kick in.] Ask prisoners at Abu Ghraib—or your last desperate moment—how fast "never" becomes "just this once."

Assumption: "This will never happen to me."
Reality: Probability doesn’t care about your confidence. The Titanic’s builders called it "unsinkable"; 1,500 corpses disagreed. Your "never" is just a failure of imagination. [Taleb’s Black Swan: Humans systematically ignore low-probability, high-impact disasters until they’re drowning in them.]

Assumption: "I’ll never forgive them."
Reality: Resentment is a prison where you’re the only inmate. fMRI scans show holding grudges lights up the anterior cingulate cortex—the same region activated by physical pain. [Emory University, 2017.] Congrats: Your "never" is a self-inflicted wound.

Unlearning Challenge:
What’s the one "never" you’ve already broken—and what’s your excuse for the next one?
